sections.content: I'm now at the red diamond. I need to move it to (3, 21) to collect all 3 red boxes. Let me check my current position first, then calculate the moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:

<function=python>
<parameter=code>

</parameter>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Player is at (30, 39), the red diamond center. I need to move to (3, 21).
- UP: (30-3)/3 = 9
- LEFT: (39-21)/3 = 6
So: UP 9, LEFT 6.
But wait, the red diamond has diagonal arms. When I move it, the arms sweep diagonally. Let me verify that moving to (3, 21) collects all 3 red boxes.
Red diamond at (3, 21):
- Diagonal 1 (top-left to bottom-right): row + col = 24
- Diagonal 2 (top-right to bottom-left): row - col = -18
Red boxes:
- (3, 21): 3+21=24 ✓ (on D1)
- (9, 27): 9-27=-18 ✓ (on D2)
- (12, 12): 12+12=24 ✓ (on D1)
All 3 are on the diagonals from (3, 21). But the question is whether the arms are long enough. The red diamond has 38 pixels, so each arm extends about 9 cells from center.
From (3, 21):
- D1 (row+col=24): arm extends ~9 cells in each direction
  - (3, 21) to (12, 12): distance = 9 cells. This is at the edge of the arm.
  - (3, 21) to other direction: would go to negative rows, so limited.
- D2 (row-col=-18): arm extends ~9 cells in each direction
  - (3, 21) to (9, 27): distance = 6 cells. Within range.
So (12, 12) is exactly 9 cells away on D1. This should be collectible.
Let me execute UP 9, LEFT 6.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move red diamond to (3, 21) to collect all 3 red boxes
# From (30, 39): UP 9, LEFT 6
action(['UP']*9 + ['LEFT']*6)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
